a:r.a:t: This is the most powerful flow in the system and it showcases why the three-layer architecture matters. The user asks "Compare Q1/Q2 sales, research trends, write report" -- a multi-part task that no single agent can handle well. The router detects multi-step intent and routes to the coordinator agent. Here is what happens at each layer. RuntimeService receives the request, hydrates the session, and calls process_turn(). The router classifies this as a coordinator campaign because it involves multiple distinct subtasks spanning different capabilities (data analysis plus document research plus synthesis). RuntimeKernel sees that coordinator.mode is "coordinator" and delegates to KernelCoordinatorController.run() instead of QueryLoop. This is the key branching point -- the Kernel decides WHERE execution happens. The coordinator controller then runs a four-stage pipeline. Stage 1: the planner agent receives the user request plus a manifest of available worker agents and their capabilities. It outputs a JSON task plan with dependencies -- in this case, three parallel tasks: data_analyst for Q1/Q2 comparison, rag_worker for trend research, and utility for skill guidance. Stage 2: the controller batches tasks by dependency order. Tasks 1, 2, and 3 have no dependencies on each other, so they run in parallel. Each worker gets its own scoped SessionState copy -- this is critical because without session isolation, parallel workers writing to the same session would corrupt each other's state. Each worker also gets its own QueryLoop instance running in its own thread. The data_analyst worker runs in ReAct mode with Python sandbox tools. The rag_worker runs in RAG mode with document retrieval. They execute simultaneously. Stage 3: the finalizer agent receives all worker results as handoff artifacts and synthesizes a coherent report. The handoff artifact system uses typed containers -- doc_digest, research_notebook, clause_redline_inventory -- with whitelists controlling which downstream agents can consume each type. Stage 4: the verifier agent fact-checks the final output. It has access to both RAG and graph tools so it can independently verify citations. If the verifier flags issues -- missing coverage, incorrect citations, gaps in analysis -- it triggers a revision loop back to the planner. The planner receives the verifier feedback and creates targeted follow-up tasks. This loop can run up to 4 rounds, configurable via max_revision_rounds. The architectural benefit of this design is composability: you can change the planner's decomposition strategy, add new worker agent types, or modify verification logic -- all independently, without touching the other components.
